thrust_flight.label: Thrust Flight — Cessna 172 Emergency Checklist (2018+ models)

quizlet_boldface.label: Quizlet — 172S NAV 3 G1000 Bold Faces Only

faa_afh.label: FAA Airplane Flying Handbook, Chapter 18 — Emergency Procedures

procedures.name: Engine Failure During Takeoff Roll
procedures.trigger: Engine loses power while still on the runway during takeoff
procedures.why_summary: Stop the airplane as quickly as possible, then systematically shut down to prevent fire.
common_mistakes: Hesitation or denial — keep accelerating hoping engine recovers
Not braking aggressively enough
Forgetting to retract flaps if set for short-field takeoff

steps.why: Stop accelerating immediately — every foot of runway you use narrows your options.
Maximum braking to stop in shortest distance. Use remaining runway.
Prevents flaps from catching ground obstacles and plants the airplane firmly on the runway.
Cuts fuel to the engine — prevents fire risk after stopping.
Eliminates ignition source.
De-energizes the standby battery (172S G1000 specific).
Removes all electrical power — eliminates spark risk.

procedures.name: Engine Failure Immediately After Takeoff
procedures.trigger: Engine loses power after liftoff, below 1,000 ft AGL
procedures.why_summary: Pitch for safe airspeed FIRST (prevent stall/spin), shut down fuel and electrical to prevent post-crash fire, land straight ahead.
common_mistakes: The 'impossible turn' — attempting to turn back to runway below 1,000 ft AGL. Almost always fatal.
Failure to pitch down immediately — startle response is to pull back, causing stall within seconds
3-5 second recognition delay — 'total disbelief' freeze before acting
Forgetting to unlatch cabin door before touchdown
Fixating on shutdown steps instead of flying the airplane

steps.why: MOST IMPORTANT STEP. Prevents stall/spin — the #1 killer in this scenario. Override the instinct to pull back.
Cuts fuel to prevent post-crash fire. A crash is survivable; a crash with fire often is not.
Backs up the mixture cutoff — ensures no fuel reaches the engine area on impact.
Eliminates ignition source on impact.
Adjust glide path to your chosen landing area.
De-energizes standby battery.
Removes all electrical power — prevents sparks on impact.
Prevents door from jamming shut if airframe deforms on impact — critical for egress.
Do NOT attempt to turn back to the runway. There is not enough altitude to complete the turn safely.

procedures.name: Engine Failure During Flight
procedures.trigger: Loss of engine power or RPM drop at cruise altitude
procedures.why_summary: Establish best glide (maximize time aloft), then troubleshoot fuel system (the #1 cause of engine failure). If no restart, execute emergency landing.
common_mistakes: Not trimming for best glide — hold pressure, get distracted, airspeed wanders
Tunnel vision on restart without scanning for landing sites
Confusion about flooded restart procedure

steps.why: Maximizes glide distance and time aloft. Trim for this speed so you can focus on troubleshooting.
Ensures fuel valve is open — it may have been bumped to the off position.
Ensures both fuel tanks are feeding the engine — one tank may be empty.
Activates the electric backup fuel pump in case the engine-driven pump failed.
Ensures full fuel flow — mixture may have been accidentally leaned too far.
Ensures both ignition circuits are active. If propeller has stopped windmilling, START engages the starter motor.

procedures.name: Engine Fire In Flight
procedures.trigger: Smoke or flames from engine compartment, burning smell, rising engine temperature
procedures.why_summary: Cut ALL fuel to the engine immediately (starve the fire), then shut down electrical, close cabin heat (it pipes engine-area air into cabin), increase airspeed to blow out flames, and execute emergency landing.
common_mistakes: Hesitating on fuel cutoff — assessing instead of acting immediately
Forgetting cabin heat/air — this pipes fire products into the cockpit
Not increasing airspeed — counterintuitive but blows out the flames
Attempting to restart engine after fire appears out — structural damage may exist

steps.why: Immediately cuts fuel flow to the engine — starves the fire of its fuel source.
Backs up the mixture cutoff — ensures no fuel reaches the engine compartment.
Stops the electric pump from pushing fuel toward the fire.
Eliminates all electrical power — removes potential ignition sources.
Allows smoke to vent from the cabin.
The cabin heat system routes air over the exhaust manifold into the cabin. With an engine fire, this is literally piping fire products into the cockpit.
COUNTERINTUITIVE: Increasing airspeed forces more airflow through the engine cowling, which can blow out the flames. You're descending faster but fire extinguishment is the priority.
Even if fire appears out, structural damage may have occurred. Land at nearest suitable location.

procedures.name: Electrical Fire In Flight
procedures.trigger: Burning smell, smoke in cabin, acrid odor from behind instrument panel
procedures.why_summary: Kill all electrical power (including standby battery), starve fire of oxygen by closing vents, extinguish, then cautiously restore power one circuit at a time. The engine keeps running — magnetos are self-powered.
common_mistakes: Resetting popped circuit breakers — the popped breaker IS the fire protection doing its job
Opening vents too early — feeds oxygen to the fire before it's out
Restoring all power at once instead of one system at a time
Forgetting STBY BATT switch — 172S specific, older models don't have it
Panicking about losing instruments — engine still runs on magnetos, you can fly and land without electrical power

steps.why: The standby battery is a SEPARATE power source that can fuel an electrical fire even with the master off. Kill it first.
De-energizes the main battery and alternator — removes the electrical energy feeding the fire.
Starves fire of oxygen. The wiring lives behind the instrument panel — cabin airflow feeds directly into that space.
Eliminates additional airflow that could feed the fire.
Use AFTER removing electrical power — otherwise the fire reignites. Direct at the base of visible flames/smoke.
Ensures every circuit is isolated before any power restoration attempt.
Clear smoke and toxic fumes from cabin. ONLY after fire is confirmed out.
Restore airflow to clear remaining smoke.

restore_power.label: If Power Needed for Continued Flight

steps.why: A popped breaker indicates the faulty circuit. Resetting it re-energizes the fault.

steps.target: ON one at a time, with delay
steps.why: Incremental restoration lets you identify which circuit caused the fire if it recurs.

procedures.name: Spin Recovery (PARE)
procedures.trigger: Aircraft enters a spin — nose pointed down, rotating around vertical axis, airspeed low and steady
procedures.why_summary: Remove pro-spin forces (power, aileron), then stop the rotation (opposite rudder), then break the stall (elevator forward). Order is critical — each step enables the next.
common_mistakes: Applying aileron input — MOST COMMON ERROR. On a stalled wing, aileron worsens the spin.
Insufficient forward elevator — afraid to push nose down when already pointed at ground
Waiting between rudder and elevator — should be quick sequence R then E
Holding opposite rudder too long after rotation stops — risks spin in other direction
Excessive back-elevator during pullout — causes secondary stall, potentially re-entering spin
Slow, cautious control inputs — spin recovery requires brisk, decisive movements
Misidentifying spin direction — look outside, not at instruments

steps.why: Running engine produces gyroscopic precession and P-factor that sustain or worsen the spin. Removing power eliminates these pro-spin forces.
COUNTERINTUITIVE: Instinct says 'roll wings level.' But wings are stalled — aileron input on a stalled wing increases angle of attack on the already-more-stalled wing, deepening the spin.
The spin is a yaw-dominated rotation. Full opposite rudder directly opposes the yaw, disrupting the aerodynamic equilibrium sustaining the spin.
THIS is what breaks the stall. Pushing forward reduces angle of attack below critical angle, unstalling both wings. Rudder stops rotation; elevator stops the stall.
Holding opposite rudder after spin stops risks entering a spin in the opposite direction.
Aggressive back-pressure can exceed critical angle of attack again — causing secondary stall and potentially a new spin.
Return to cruise power smoothly after stable flight is established.
